markdown:
# Components of Algorithms To make an algorithm **functional and finite**, an algorithm needs to have some (but commonly all) of the following **components**: 1. Instructions 2. Variables 3. Conditions and conditional statements 4. Loops ## Instructions 1. An instruction is a **single activity** ran in an algorithm. It is usually represented by a single line of code in most programming languages. 2. Instructions on their own represent a **linear set of actions** that the algorithm **should perform while it is running**. Imagine an algorithm that explains **how to go to the store to buy some apples**. This algorithm could be **deconstructed into the following instructions** (for example): * Walk to store * Pick up apples * Pay apples at register * Return home with apples Where each of the above four is **one instruction**. ## Variables A variable is a **memory storage location** that holds the values that the_ _algorithm needs, **which may change during runtime**. Mathematics have variables similar to the ones used by computers. Consider the following mathematical expression: $$2x-2$$ Here, **x** is the **variable**, as its value is not explicitly stated and may vary. Algorithms can use variables in much the same way. They are used to **store data** that can vary (hence the name “variables”) during execution, and/or that are unknown during development (for example, the age of the user). Imagine that we need to store the **user’s age**, which we don’t know during development, and which varies based on the user who is running the algorithm. 
input AGE
This above short code example would be an **instruction** that allows a user to input their age into the **variable** called AGE. ## Conditionals 1. A conditional, also known as a **condition **or **conditional statement**, is a component of an algorithm that allows for a certain set of instructions to be run **only if a specific condition has been met**. 2. This allows developers to introduce **logic **and **algorithmic decisions** in their algorithms. To continue the algorithm from the previous section about variables, let’s say that we need our algorithm to **only** allow the user to use the algorithm **if they are of legal age** (over the age of 18). 
input AGE
if AGE > 17 then
    output “Welcome!”
else
    output “You must be at least 18!”
Algorithms may have **multiple conditionals**, and a single conditional can have **multiple individual conditions**, or just one, depending on the needs of the algorithm. ## Loops 1. A loop is an algorithm component that allows a set of instructions **to run multiple times**. 2. There are **two main types of loops**, which are: 1. “For” loops repeat a set of instructions a **specific limited number of times.** 2. “While” loops keep repeating a set of instructions **for as long as a given condition is met.** Conditionals and while loops **both **have a set of instructions that they run if a certain condition is met, but the main difference is that a conditional runs the instructions **only once** if the condition is met, whereas while loops will run the instructions **repeatedly** while the condition is still met.
